raw_evidence.description: A series of 34 rock-cut cave temples and monasteries dating from the 6th to 11th century CE, representing Buddhist, Hindu and Jain religious traditions carved directly into the Charanandri Hills. The caves contain intricate sculptures, reliefs and frescoes depicting deities, celestial beings and scenes from religious texts.

extracted_evidence.historic_features: 34 rock-cut cave temples spanning Buddhist, Hindu and Jain traditions, constructed between 6th–11th century CE. Kailasa Temple (Cave 16) is the largest monolithic rock excavation in the world, carved from a single basalt cliff.
extracted_evidence.cultural_significance: Unique example of religious tolerance — three distinct faiths represented in adjacent caves over five centuries. The site demonstrates the coexistence of Buddhist Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions with Brahmanical Hinduism and Digambara Jainism.
extracted_evidence.integrity: The property is largely intact and well-preserved within a protected area buffer zone. All 34 caves are fully included in the boundary with no modern encroachment.
extracted_evidence.authenticity: Form, materials, and design are authentic. The rock-cut structures are original fabric, and conservation work uses traditional techniques and indigenous materials.
extracted_evidence.geographic_context: Located in the Deccan Plateau, Maharashtra, India. Caves are carved into the volcanic basalt escarpment of the Charanandri Hills. The natural setting is highly significant.
extracted_evidence.documentation_quality: Extensive scholarly documentation since colonial surveys (1819). ASI maintains detailed records. Multiple academic publications available.
extracted_evidence.management_protection: Protected by national legislation under the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and covered by a comprehensive site management plan.
extracted_evidence.supporting_evidence: Three on-site photographs provided showing cave exteriors, interior sculpture and Kailasa Temple facade.

scoring.rationale: Heritage Score: 93/100 (High Confidence). Historic Features: 22/25 — Cultural Significance: 18/20 — Integrity: 12/15 — Authenticity: 12/15 — Geographic Context: 8/10 — Documentation: 8/10 — Management & Protection: 3/5 — Supporting Evidence: 10/15.
